primary_diagnosis.name: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (suspected Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome or AVNRT), currently sustained and haemodynamically stable

pathophysiology_summary: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) is a re-entrant tachyarrhythmia producing abrupt-onset, regular, rapid rates (often 150-220 bpm). In a young patient with a structurally normal heart, the common mechanisms are atrioventricular nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) or an accessory-pathway-mediated re-entry; Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome refers to a short PR interval (<120 ms) with a normal narrow QRS (no delta wave, distinguishing it from Wolff-Parkinson-White) and a predisposition to paroxysmal SVT via enhanced AV nodal conduction. The abrupt onset/offset and perfect regularity distinguish SVT from sinus tachycardia (gradual, rate proportionate to stimulus) and atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular); a young stable patient conversing at 180 bpm makes ventricular tachycardia less likely, though a broad-complex tachycardia would change this. Caffeine lowers the arrhythmia threshold. While SVT in a structurally normal heart is usually not immediately life-threatening, a sustained fast rate can cause presyncope, and prolonged tachycardia risks fatigue/decompensation; haemodynamic instability (hypotension, severe chest pain, syncope, pulmonary oedema) would mandate emergency (including synchronised cardioversion). First-line termination is a vagal manoeuvre (a modified Valsalva is most effective); if unsuccessful, pharmacological termination with intravenous adenosine under ECG monitoring is used, so a 12-lead ECG capturing the rhythm in tachycardia and monitored treatment are needed. These require in-person assessment; a vagal manoeuvre can be safely coached over telehealth, but adenosine/cardioversion cannot be given remotely, so urgent in-person assessment is required, escalating to emergency if unstable.

differential.diagnosis: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (AVNRT/accessory pathway, e.g. LGL)
differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt-onset, regular, very fast palpitations at rest in a young person are classic for SVT.
Anxiety/caffeine can raise heart rate, but the abrupt onset and rate of ~180 at rest are out of proportion and more consistent with SVT.
Considered for palpitations, but a perfectly regular rhythm argues against the irregularly irregular pulse of AF.
Must be kept in mind for any sustained tachycardia, though a young structurally-normal, stable, conversing patient favours SVT; a broad-complex tachycardia on ECG would change this.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise probable SVT, assess haemodynamic stability, and coach a vagal manoeuvre while arranging urgent ECG capture.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt regular ~180 bpm tachycardia (DI-001) sustained >30 min (DI-002) with presyncope (DI-003) but no severe chest pain/syncope (DI-004) in a young caffeine-exposed patient indicates stable SVT needing vagal manoeuvre then urgent ECG/adenosine.
Abrupt onset and disproportionate rate at rest are not sinus tachycardia.
Remains a consideration until the ECG confirms a narrow-complex tachycardia; haemodynamic stability and youth favour SVT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is stable SVT: coach a vagal manoeuvre; if unsuccessful, arrange urgent in-person ECG and adenosine, escalating to emergency if unstable.

differential.diagnosis: Paroxysmal SVT (suspected LGL/AVNRT) - requiring 12-lead ECG in tachycardia and monitored termination
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Definitive rhythm diagnosis needs a 12-lead ECG captured during tachycardia; vagal manoeuvres can be coached, but adenosine/cardioversion require in-person monitored care.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is stable SVT - coach a vagal manoeuvre, arrange urgent in-person ECG/adenosine if it persists, and escalate to 000 if unstable.

red_flags.description: Abrupt-onset, regular, very rapid tachycardia (~180 bpm) at rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates a paroxysmal re-entrant SVT requiring rhythm capture and termination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated sustained tachycardia causes ongoing presyncope and risks decompensation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A vagal-refractory, sustained episode needs prompt medical termination (ECG-guided adenosine).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prolonged tachycardia leads to fatigue and possible haemodynamic decline.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic-instability features (severe chest pain, syncope, hypotension) - currently ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: If any develop, the SVT is unstable and requires emergency care (including synchronised cardioversion); their absence supports stable management with vagal manoeuvre and urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If instability develops and is not acted on, cardiovascular collapse can occur.

red_flags.description: Short PR interval (<120 ms) with a normal narrow QRS and no delta wave on 12-lead ECG (LGL pattern), rhythm captured in tachycardia
red_flags.clinical_significance: The ECG (short PR, narrow QRS without delta wave, and the tachycardia morphology) defines the arrhythmia mechanism and cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires a 12-lead ECG; the correct action is urgent in-person rhythm capture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anxiety and caffeine can contribute, but an abrupt regular ~180 bpm at rest is SVT, not simple sinus tachycardia, and needs rhythm capture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to coach a vagal manoeuvre
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A modified Valsalva is a safe, effective first-line telehealth-coachable intervention for stable SVT that is often missed.
The AI Doctor must screen for chest pain, syncope and hypotension, which would upgrade this to an emergency needing cardioversion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising the patient to drive himself
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Driving while tachycardic and presyncopal risks collapse at the wheel; assisted transport or ambulance is safer.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG capturing the rhythm in tachycardia (short PR, narrow QRS, no delta wave)
Accurate clinician-measured heart rate and blood pressure during the episode
Administration of intravenous adenosine or synchronised cardioversion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Coach a safe vagal manoeuvre (modified Valsalva) first; if the SVT persists, arrange urgent in-person assessment (emergency department) for a 12-lead ECG in tachycardia and monitored pharmacological termination (adenosine); advise assisted transport (not self-driving), and call 000 if severe chest pain, worsening breathlessness, or near-syncope develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing a sustained 180 bpm regular tachycardia to anxiety/caffeine and advising the patient to wait, or to drive himself, risks prolonged tachycardia, presyncope and a collapse at the wheel, and misses an ECG rhythm capture.
